findings.title: Telnet Service Active - Replace with SSH Immediately
findings.description: Telnet transmits ALL data including passwords in plaintext. Any network observer can capture credentials with Wireshark. This is considered a critical vulnerability on any production device.
findings.fix:
1. Disable Telnet service immediately.
2. Install SSH: apt-get install openssh-server
3. For routers/switches: access via SSH or web console.
4. If this is a router, update firmware.

findings.title: Dropbear SSH 0.51 Detected
findings.description: Dropbear SSH 0.51 is in use. Dropbear SSH 0.51 - old/vulnerable build Dropbear is a lightweight SSH server common on routers and IoT devices.
findings.fix:
Update Dropbear SSH to the latest release.
For embedded devices: check manufacturer firmware updates.
If this is a router (192.168.x.x), update its firmware.

findings.title: Unencrypted HTTP on Port 80
findings.description: Web service running without TLS. All traffic including session tokens and passwords transmitted in plaintext.
findings.fix:
Configure HTTPS with TLS 1.2+.
Redirect all HTTP to HTTPS.
Free cert: certbot --nginx or certbot --apache

findings.title: Unencrypted Protocol - Port 21 (FTP)
findings.description: FTP transmits credentials and data in plaintext. Any network observer can capture all traffic (Wireshark, tcpdump).
findings.fix: FTP - credentials in plaintext. Replace with SFTP (port 22) or FTPS.

findings.title: Unencrypted Protocol - Port 23 (Telnet)
findings.description: Telnet transmits credentials and data in plaintext. Any network observer can capture all traffic (Wireshark, tcpdump).
findings.fix: Telnet - all traffic unencrypted. Replace with SSH immediately.

findings.title: Unencrypted Protocol - Port 80 (HTTP)
findings.description: HTTP transmits credentials and data in plaintext. Any network observer can capture all traffic (Wireshark, tcpdump).
findings.fix: HTTP - redirect to HTTPS. Get free cert: certbot --nginx.

findings.title: SSH Port 22 Exposed - Brute Force Risk
findings.description: SSH on port 22 is constantly scanned by automated bots. Brute-force and credential stuffing attacks are ongoing.
findings.fix:
1. Change SSH port from 22 to a high port (e.g. 2222).
2. PermitRootLogin no  in /etc/ssh/sshd_config
3. PasswordAuthentication no  (use key-based auth only)
4. Install fail2ban: apt-get install fail2ban
5. Restrict to known IPs: AllowUsers user@trusted_ip
